summary:
Ivan IV 'the Terrible' (r. 1547–1584 AD) was the first Russian ruler to take the title 'Tsar of all Russia,' a figure of extremes whose reign combined genuine modernizing reforms with episodes of horrifying personal violence, and whose Oprichnina terror — a state within a state operating under his personal command — inaugurated a pattern of autocratic, personalized rule and mass political violence that would recur throughout Russian history. Ivan's early reign (the 'good period') was constructive: he convened Russia's first representative assembly (the Zemsky Sobor), created a standing army (the streltsy), reformed the law code (the Sudebnik of 1550), expanded trade with England (through the newly discovered northern sea route), and conquered the Khanates of Kazan (1552) and Astrakhan (1556), expanding Russian territory dramatically to the east and south.

The Oprichnina period (1565–1572) revealed the pathological dimension of Ivan's character. After a mysterious voluntary abdication that the boyar council begged him to reverse, Ivan returned to power with the authority to create a separate court (oprichnina) under his direct personal control, staffed by black-robed horsemen (oprichniki) who could arrest, torture, and execute anyone accused of treason without the normal judicial processes. The massacre of Novgorod (1570), in which Ivan personally supervised the slaughter of an estimated 2,000–15,000 of the city's inhabitants over five weeks on suspicion of treasonable contact with Poland-Lithuania, was the Oprichnina's most horrifying episode. Ivan killed his own son and heir Ivan Ivanovich in a rage (1581), an act he immediately regretted and mourned for the rest of his life. His legacy was deeply contradictory: territorial expansion and state consolidation alongside the devastation of Russia's administrative capacity and the traumatization of the landowning class that would contribute to the [[event:2411|Time of Troubles]].
